records.content:
This project covered data cleaning, exploratory analysis, and feature engineering for sales order data.

Key outcomes included:

improved data quality

better understanding of order patterns

creation of useful analytical features
This project explores ERP sales order data using Python.

Clean and prepare raw sales data

Perform exploratory data analysis

Engineer useful features for downstream analysis

Python

Pandas

NumPy

Matplotlib / Seaborn

Jupyter Notebook

## This dataset is the sales data from SharpLight Technologies January 2024 to October 2025
## Which is 1 year and 10 months. We would like to see the health of the sales and forscast 
## sales for the next year



# For Data Manipulation
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# For Visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# For Error Handling 
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")



# File Paths
excel_path = "C:/Users/aemil\OneDrive/Desktop/Portfolio/sales-orders-analytics/data/Raw/SalesOrders.xlsx"  
csv_path = "C:/Users/aemil\OneDrive/Desktop/Portfolio/sales-orders-analytics/data/Raw/SalesOrders.csv" 

# Try loading Excel first thrn CSV
try:
    df = pd.read_excel(excel_path)
    source = "excel"
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Excel load failed: {e}")
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
    source = "csv"

print(f"Data loaded successfully from {source}")




df.shape



df.columns



df.dtypes



df.head()



df.tail()



# Due date stored as object instead of datetime
# Date of excecution stored as object instead of datetime
# Columns with no values, to be dropped : Sale Type, Description of Type, Item Centre, Source of Opp, Leads Group Name, Details, Remark and Remark 2
# Missing values for Date of execution and Country, which impact time based and geographic analysis



# Missing values count per column
missing_count = df.isna().sum()



missing_count



missing_pct = (missing_count / len(df)) * 100



missing_pct



missing_summary = (
    pd.DataFrame({
        "missing_count": missing_count,
        "missing_pct": missing_pct
    })
    .sort_values("missing_pct", ascending=False)
)



missing_summary



df.duplicated().sum()



df.describe()



# All completly empty columns

couls_to_drop = [
    "Sale Type",
    "Description of Type",
    "Deal Type",	
    "Item center",
    "Source of Opp",
    "Leads Group Name",
    "Details",
    "Remark 1",
    "Remark 2"
]

df_clean = df.drop(columns=couls_to_drop, errors="ignore")




df_clean.head()



# Fixing Date Columns 
date_couls = ["Due Date", "Date of Execution"]

for col in date_couls:
    df_clean[col] = pd.to_datetime(df_clean[col], errors="coerce")




##df_clean.dtypes



##df_clean.head()



# All duplicate rows (show every copy)
dupes_all = df_clean[df_clean.duplicated(keep=False)]
dupes_all




# Count of duplicates by business key (Order + Part Number)
duplicate_counts = (
    df_clean.groupby(["Order", "Part Number"])
    .size()   # count occurrences
    .reset_index(name='count')
    .query('count > 1')  # only duplicates
)



duplicate_counts



df_clean_1 = df_clean.copy()



numeric_cols = ['Quantity', 'Balance', 'Total Price', 'Value of Balance']



df_clean = (
    df_clean.groupby(['Order', 'Part Number'], as_index=False)
    .agg(
        {**{col: 'sum' for col in numeric_cols},  # sum numeric columns
         **{col: 'last' for col in df_clean.columns if col not in numeric_cols + ['Order', 'Part Number']}}
    )
)



#df_clean = df_clean.drop_duplicates(subset=["Order", "Part Number"], keep='last').reset_index(drop=True)



df_clean["Country"] = df_clean["Country"].fillna("Unknown")



df_clean["is_executed"] = df_clean["Order Status"].isin(["Paid", "Confirmed"])



df_clean.head(30)



df_clean.info()
df_clean.isna().sum()





import os
file_path = "C:/Users/aemil/OneDrive/Desktop/Portfolio/sales-orders-analytics/data/processed/sales_orders_clean.csv"
df_clean.to_csv(file_path, index=False)
if os.path.exists(file_path):
    print("Successfully saved")
else:
    print ("Save failed")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# for plotting
plt.style.use("default")
sns.set_theme(context="notebook", style="whitegrid")



df = pd.read_csv("C:/Users/aemil/OneDrive/Desktop/Portfolio/sales-orders-analytics/data/processed/sales_orders_clean.csv")



df.shape



df.head()



df.info()



summary = (
    df
    .groupby("is_executed")
    .agg(
        number_of_orders=("Order", "nunique"),
        total_revenue=("Total Price ($)", "sum")
    )
    .reset_index()
)

summary



plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))

sns.barplot(
    data=summary,
    x="is_executed",
    y="total_revenue",
    palette=["darkgrey", "#800080"]
)

plt.title("Executed vs Pipeline Revenue")
plt.xlabel("Order Executed")
plt.ylabel("Total Revenue ($)")

plt.xticks(
    ticks=[0, 1],
    labels=["Draft Deal/Potential Deal", "Deal Won"]
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



df.columns



top_customers = (
    df.groupby('Customer Name')['Total Price ($)']
    .sum()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(5)
)
print(top_customers)



top_customers.plot(kind='barh', color='purple', figsize=(8,5))
plt.xlabel("Total Revenue ($)")
plt.title("Top 5 Customers by Revenue")
plt.gca().invert_yaxis() 
plt.show()



order_values = (
    df
    .groupby("Order")
    .agg(order_total_value=("Total Price ($)", "sum"))
    .reset_index()
)



plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))

sns.histplot(
    order_values["order_total_value"],
    bins=30,
    kde=True,
    log_scale=True
)

plt.title("Distribution of Order Total Value")
plt.xlabel("Order Total Value ($)")
plt.ylabel("Number of Orders")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



# Correctly used a log scale : Small and large orders are both visible and outliers don’t flatten the rest of the data
# Most orders cluster in the low–mid thousands
# A small number of very large orders stretch far to the right (10⁴–10⁵)
# Averages will lie to you here : 
    ## Mean order value will be pulled up
    ## Median will be far more representative of a “typical” order
# The data is messy.. there are a lot of test orders, owing orders to customers, etc.
# Consumables are bought more frequently than machines and handpices being used for 1-2 yrs



sales_rep_summary = (
    df
    .groupby(["Sales Rep Name", "is_executed"])
    .agg(
        total_revenue=("Total Price ($)", "sum"),
        order_count=("Order", "nunique")
    )
    .reset_index()
)



sales_rep_pivot = (
    rep_summary
    .pivot(
        index="Sales Rep Name",
        columns="is_executed",
        values="total_revenue"
    )
    .fillna(0)
    .reset_index()
)



sales_rep_pivot.columns = [
    "Sales Rep Name",
    "Pipeline Value",
    "Executed Revenue"
]



sales_rep_pivot["total_exposure"] = (
    sales_rep_pivot["Pipeline Value"] + sales_rep_pivot["Executed Revenue"]
)

sales_rep_pivot["pipeline_ratio"] = (
    sales_rep_pivot["Pipeline Value"] / sales_rep_pivot["total_exposure"]
)



sales_rep_analysis = sales_rep_pivot.sort_values(
    "pipeline_ratio",
    ascending=False
)



plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

sns.barplot(
    data=sales_rep_analysis,
    x="pipeline_ratio",
    y="Sales Rep Name",
    color="purple"
)

plt.title("Draft Risk by Sales Rep")
plt.xlabel("Pipeline Ratio (Draft / Total Value)")
plt.ylabel("Sales Rep")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




# Bearing in mind that this means the deal wasnt closed within 16 months 
# High pipeline here may signal process gaps, not performance gaps

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# for plotting
plt.style.use("default")
sns.set_theme(context="notebook", style="whitegrid")



import os



df = pd.read_csv("C:/Users/aemil/OneDrive/Desktop/Portfolio/sales-orders-analytics/data/processed/sales_orders_clean.csv")



order_features = (
    df
    .groupby("Order")
    .agg(
        order_total_value=("Total Price ($)", "sum"),
        order_line_count=("Part Number", "count"),

        # Quantity = fulfillment state
        order_quantity_net=("Quantity", "sum"),
        has_unshipped_items=("Quantity", lambda x: (x < 0).any()),

        # Balance = financial exposure
        balance_net=("Balance", "sum"),
        customer_owes=("Balance", lambda x: (x > 0).any()),
        company_owes=("Balance", lambda x: (x < 0).any()),

        # Execution & identity
        is_executed=("is_executed", "max"),
        customer_number=("Customer Number", "first"),
        customer_name=("Customer Name", "first"),
        sales_rep=("Sales Rep Name", "first"),
        order_status=("Order Status", "first")
    )
    .reset_index()
)



order_features[["balance_net", "customer_owes", "company_owes"]].head()



order_features[["order_quantity_net", "has_unshipped_items"]].head()



order_features



customer_features = (
    order_features
    .groupby("customer_number")
    .agg(
        customer_name=("customer_name", "first"),

        # Volume & value
        customer_total_revenue=("order_total_value", "sum"),
        customer_avg_order_value=("order_total_value", "mean"),
        customer_order_count=("Order", "nunique"),

        # Execution behavior
        customer_execution_rate=("is_executed", "mean"),

        # Financial exposure
        customer_balance_net=("balance_net", "sum"),
        customer_owes_ratio=("customer_owes", "mean"),
        company_owes_ratio=("company_owes", "mean"),

        # Fulfillment behavior
        unshipped_rate=("has_unshipped_items", "mean"),
        avg_order_line_count=("order_line_count", "mean")
    )
    .reset_index()
)




customer_features[["unshipped_rate"]].describe()



customer_features["unshipped_rate"].value_counts(bins=5)



customer_features



rep_features = (
    order_features
    .groupby("sales_rep")
    .agg(
        rep_total_revenue=("order_total_value", "sum"),
        rep_avg_order_value=("order_total_value", "mean"),
        rep_order_count=("Order", "nunique"),

        # Execution performance
        rep_execution_rate=("is_executed", "mean"),

        # Pipeline / financial exposure
        rep_balance_net=("balance_net", "sum"),
        rep_customer_owes_ratio=("customer_owes", "mean"),
        rep_company_owes_ratio=("company_owes", "mean"),

        # Operational signal (fulfillment pressure)
        rep_unshipped_rate=("has_unshipped_items", "mean"),

        # Complexity proxy
        rep_avg_line_count=("order_line_count", "mean")
    )
    .reset_index()
)



rep_features.sort_values("rep_total_revenue", ascending=False).head()
rep_features.describe()



##rep_features[["rep_execution_rate", "rep_unshipped_rate"]].sort_values("rep_execution_rate").reset_index(drop=True)



#sanity check 1
rep_features.loc[0]



#sanity check 2
rep_features.sort_values("rep_execution_rate").head()



#sanity check 3
rep_features.sort_values("rep_execution_rate")[[
    "sales_rep",
    "rep_order_count",
    "rep_execution_rate",
    "rep_unshipped_rate"
]].head(10)



rep_features["rep_order_count"].describe()



rep_features_filtered = rep_features[
    rep_features["rep_order_count"] >= 5
]



## this filter can be adjusted, saved as CSV and used for any business purposes 
rep_features_filtered



#we will now export customer_features as a CSV for further analysis
file_path = "../data/processed/customer_features.csv"
customer_features.to_csv(file_path, index=False)

if os.path.exists(file_path):
    print("Customer features exported successfully.")
else:
    print("Export failed.")
